agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Bird's 30-second staleness rule creates a fundamental tension with the proposed PostgreSQL-only architecture. Courier location is ephemeral, high-frequency data — writing every update to a relational database adds unnecessary latency, I/O pressure, and table bloat for data that expires in 30 seconds. The recommended architecture introduces a hot/warm split: Redis (or a similar in-memory store) serves as the real-time location layer enforcing the staleness invariant, while PostgreSQL serves as the durable store for historical location trails (warm/cold data). This preserves the team's existing PostgreSQL investment while honoring the domain rule without compromise.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Location Ingestion Service","responsibility":"Receives GPS pings from courier devices via WebSocket or gRPC stream. Validates payload (lat, lng, courier_id, device_timestamp). Publishes validated location events.","boundaries":"Owns the ingestion protocol and input validation. Does NOT enforce staleness — that is a read-side concern."},{"name":"Real-Time Location Store (Redis)","responsibility":"Holds the latest location per courier as a key-value pair with a TTL of 30 seconds. This is the ONLY read source for customer-facing location queries. If the key has expired, no location is returned — enforcing Bird's staleness rule at the infrastructure level.","boundaries":"Ephemeral data only. No history. No queries beyond single-courier lookup. TTL is the staleness enforcement mechanism."},{"name":"Location History Store (PostgreSQL)","responsibility":"Persists location events for historical analytics, route reconstruction, dispute resolution, and operational reporting. Writes are asynchronous and batched — not on the critical path for customer reads.","boundaries":"Owns the durable, queryable history. NOT used for real-time customer-facing reads."},{"name":"Location Query API","responsibility":"Serves customer-facing requests for courier position. Reads exclusively from Redis. Returns either a valid location or a 'location unavailable' response when the key is absent/expired.","boundaries":"Read-only. Thin layer. No business logic beyond formatting the response."},{"name":"Location Event Bus","responsibility":"Decouples ingestion from storage. Location events are published once and consumed by both the Redis writer and the PostgreSQL batch writer independently.","boundaries":"At-least-once delivery. Consumers are idempotent."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Courier Device","to":"Location Ingestion Service","contract":"WebSocket or gRPC stream: { courier_id: UUID, lat: float, lng: float, timestamp: ISO8601, accuracy_m: float }","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (versioned schema)"},{"from":"Location Ingestion Service","to":"Location Event Bus","contract":"Publish LocationUpdated event with validated payload","coupling_type":"event coupling (loose)"},{"from":"Location Event Bus","to":"Real-Time Location Store (Redis)","contract":"Consumer sets key courier:{id} with 30s TTL containing latest lat/lng/timestamp","coupling_type":"event coupling (loose)"},{"from":"Location Event Bus","to":"Location History Store (PostgreSQL)","contract":"Consumer batch-inserts rows into courier_locations table asynchronously","coupling_type":"event coupling (loose)"},{"from":"Location Query API","to":"Real-Time Location Store (Redis)","contract":"GET courier:{id} → { lat, lng, timestamp } or null","coupling_type":"runtime dependency (synchronous read)"},{"from":"Customer App","to":"Location Query API","contract":"GET /couriers/{id}/location → 200 { lat, lng, updated_at } or 204 No Content (stale/unavailable)","coupling_type":"REST API (versioned)"}],"data_flow":"Courier device → WebSocket/gRPC → Location Ingestion Service → validates → publishes to Event Bus → two independent consumers: (1) Redis writer sets key with 30s TTL for real-time reads, (2) PostgreSQL writer batch-inserts for history. Customer app → Location Query API → reads Redis → returns location or 'unavailable'. The 30s staleness rule is enforced by Redis TTL expiry — no application-level timestamp checking needed on the read path.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Write path (ingestion → event bus → stores) is fully separated from read path (API → Redis). This allows independent optimization: writes can be batched for PostgreSQL while reads are sub-millisecond from Redis."},{"pattern":"TTL-based Staleness Enforcement","rationale":"Instead of checking timestamps in application code on every read (error-prone, clock-skew sensitive), the 30-second invariant is enforced structurally by Redis key expiry. If the key exists, it's fresh. If not, it's stale. The domain rule becomes impossible to violate."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Decouples ingestion from storage concerns. Adding new consumers (e.g., geofence alerting, ETA calculation) requires zero changes to the ingestion service."},{"pattern":"Hot/Warm/Cold Data Separation","rationale":"Real-time location (hot) lives in memory. Recent history (warm) in PostgreSQL. Old history (cold) can be archived or partitioned. Each tier is optimized for its access pattern."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Courier Tracking (Real-Time)","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierLocation — not a traditional aggregate; modeled as a continuously-updated projection. Identity: courier_id. Invariant: location is never older than 30 seconds when served to customers."]},{"name":"Courier Tracking (History)","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["LocationTrail — append-only sequence of location events per courier. Used for analytics and dispute resolution, not real-time display."]},{"name":"Delivery Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery — consumes courier location as a read-only input for ETA calculation and geofence triggers. Does not own location data."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Courier Tracking (Real-Time)","downstream":"Delivery Management","relationship":"Open Host Service — Location Query API provides a stable interface for downstream consumers. Delivery Management is a conformist consumer."},{"upstream":"Courier Tracking (Real-Time)","downstream":"Courier Tracking (History)","relationship":"Event-driven — History context subscribes to the same location events. No direct coupling. Anti-Corruption Layer not needed since both share the same source event schema."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Introduce Redis as the real-time location store instead of PostgreSQL-only","gains":"Sub-millisecond reads. Structural enforcement of 30s staleness via TTL. No table bloat from ephemeral data. Clear separation of real-time vs historical concerns.","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure component (Redis). Team must operate and monitor Redis. Slight increase in system complexity.","alternatives_considered":["PostgreSQL-only with timestamp filtering on read — rejected because it pushes staleness enforcement to application code (every query must include WHERE timestamp > now() - 30s), is vulnerable to clock skew, creates table bloat from high-frequency inserts of throwaway data, and adds unnecessary I/O load for ephemeral state.","PostgreSQL with LISTEN/NOTIFY for real-time push — rejected because NOTIFY is not designed for high-frequency per-courier updates and lacks TTL semantics. Still requires polling or complex pub/sub wiring.","In-memory application cache only — rejected because it doesn't survive restarts and creates consistency issues across multiple API server instances."]},{"decision":"Event bus between ingestion and storage","gains":"Decoupled consumers. Easy to add new location-reactive features. Independent scaling of write vs read paths. Replay capability.","sacrifices":"Adds latency (milliseconds) between ingestion and availability. Requires event bus infrastructure (e.g., Kafka, Redis Streams, or even PostgreSQL-based pg_notify for lower scale).","alternatives_considered":["Direct synchronous writes from ingestion to both Redis and PostgreSQL — rejected because it couples ingestion throughput to the slowest consumer and makes the system brittle under load."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Event Bus Implementation","evolution_path":"Start with Redis Streams (since Redis is already in the stack) for simplicity. Migrate to Kafka if event volume or consumer count grows significantly."},{"area":"Location Query API","evolution_path":"Currently REST. Can evolve to Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSocket push for true real-time streaming to customer apps without changing the backend storage model."},{"area":"Historical Storage","evolution_path":"PostgreSQL with time-based partitioning initially. Can migrate to TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) or a dedicated time-series database if query patterns demand it."},{"area":"Staleness Threshold","evolution_path":"The 30-second TTL is a configuration value in the Redis writer consumer. Changing it requires zero code changes — just a config update and consumer restart."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Staleness Rule Enforcement via TTL","reason":"This is intentionally rigid. The 30-second rule is a domain invariant from Bird. Enforcing it structurally (TTL) rather than in application code means it cannot be accidentally bypassed by a careless query. This rigidity is a feature."},{"area":"Redis as the Single Source for Real-Time Reads","reason":"All customer-facing location reads MUST go through Redis, never PostgreSQL. This constraint must be enforced at the API layer and documented as a team convention. Violating it breaks the staleness guarantee."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Redis failure causes total loss of real-time location visibility for customers","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Run Redis in a clustered/sentinel configuration with automatic failover. Location Query API should return 204 (unavailable) gracefully on Redis connection failure — never fall back to PostgreSQL (which would serve stale data and violate Bird's rule). Monitor Redis health with sub-second alerting."},{"risk":"Clock skew between courier devices and server causes premature or delayed staleness","severity":"medium","mitigation":"TTL is set server-side at write time, not based on device timestamp. The 30-second window starts when the server receives the update, not when the device generated it. Device timestamps are stored for diagnostics but not used for staleness enforcement."},{"risk":"High courier volume overwhelms Redis write throughput","severity":"low","mitigation":"Redis handles ~100K+ SET operations per second on commodity hardware. Even 10,000 couriers updating every 5 seconds is only 2,000 writes/sec. Well within capacity. Monitor and shard if needed."},{"risk":"PostgreSQL batch writer falls behind under load","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Since PostgreSQL writes are not on the critical path, temporary lag is acceptable. Use a dead-letter queue for failed batches. Partition the courier_locations table by time (daily/weekly) to manage table size."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Deploy Redis alongside existing PostgreSQL. Implement the Location Ingestion Service writing to Redis (SET with 30s TTL) and PostgreSQL (batch INSERT). Build Location Query API reading from Redis only. Phase 2: Introduce event bus to decouple ingestion from consumers. Phase 3: Add SSE/WebSocket push to customer apps for true streaming updates.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/services/location-ingestion-service.ts — WebSocket/gRPC handler for device pings","src/services/location-redis-writer.ts — writes to Redis with TTL","src/services/location-pg-writer.ts — batch writes to PostgreSQL","src/api/routes/courier-location.ts — GET endpoint reading from Redis","src/config/redis.ts — Redis client configuration","migrations/XXXX_create_courier_locations.sql — PostgreSQL table with timestamp index and partitioning","infrastructure/redis.yml — Redis deployment configuration"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the existing service patterns in the codebase. Redis client should be injected as a dependency for testability. PostgreSQL writer should use batch inserts (multi-row INSERT) rather than individual statements. API should return 204 No Content (not an error) when location is unavailable — this is a normal state, not an exception.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER fall back to PostgreSQL reads when Redis is unavailable — this would serve stale data and violate the domain rule. Return 'unavailable' instead.","Do NOT store location history in Redis — it's not designed for unbounded growth per key. Redis is for current state only.","Do NOT use application-level timestamp checks as the primary staleness mechanism — TTL is more reliable and cannot be accidentally omitted from a query.","Do NOT use PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY as a substitute for proper event infrastructure — it doesn't scale and has no replay capability.","Avoid updating PostgreSQL synchronously on the hot path — it will become the bottleneck under load."]},"escalations":[{"type":"domain_architecture_conflict","description":"Bird's rule states location 'must be updated in real-time' and readings older than 30 seconds 'must not be displayed.' A PostgreSQL-only architecture creates a conflict: (1) High-frequency writes to PostgreSQL for ephemeral data that expires in 30 seconds is architecturally wasteful — most rows are written only to be immediately irrelevant. (2) Enforcing the 30-second staleness rule in SQL (WHERE updated_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 seconds') is fragile — any query that omits this clause silently violates the domain invariant. (3) Under load, PostgreSQL write latency could push the effective staleness beyond 30 seconds, violating the rule at the infrastructure level. The domain rule demands structural enforcement, not best-effort application code.","options":["Option A: PostgreSQL-only with disciplined WHERE clauses — simple infrastructure, but staleness rule is enforced by convention (fragile). Every new query must remember the filter. Table bloat from ephemeral data.","Option B: Redis for real-time + PostgreSQL for history (recommended) — adds Redis to the stack but enforces staleness structurally via TTL. Impossible to serve stale data. Clean separation of concerns.","Option C: PostgreSQL with materialized view or function abstraction — hides the WHERE clause behind a view/function, but still stores ephemeral data in PostgreSQL and doesn't prevent direct table access."],"recommendation":"Option B. Bird's rule is a hard invariant, not a guideline. It deserves structural enforcement. The additional operational cost of Redis is justified by the guarantee that stale data is physically impossible to serve. Recommend discussing with Bird to confirm that 'must not be displayed' means a hard guarantee (structural enforcement) vs best-effort (application code)."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["The hot/warm data split pattern is well-established for real-time location tracking","Redis TTL as structural staleness enforcement is the industry-standard approach","Event-driven decoupling of ingestion from storage is appropriate at any reasonable scale"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the team has Redis operational expertise and is willing to add it to the stack","The exact scale (number of couriers, update frequency) which affects whether Redis Streams vs Kafka is the right event bus choice","Whether Bird's 'must not be displayed' is an absolute invariant or has acceptable degradation modes (e.g., during Redis outage, is 'unavailable' acceptable or must a fallback exist?)"],"assumptions":["Bird's 30-second rule is a hard invariant requiring structural enforcement, not just application-level best effort","The team is open to adding Redis to the infrastructure if the architectural justification is sound","Courier devices send GPS updates at least every 15-20 seconds under normal conditions","Customer-facing reads need sub-second latency","The existing PostgreSQL instance should be preserved for historical/analytical queries"]}}

Courier location is ephemeral, high-frequency data — writing every update to a relational database adds unnecessary latency, I/O pressure, and table bloat for data that expires in 30 seconds.
